case_name: River water treatment for TCP and MTBE prior to groundwater recharge under moderate cost and limited-operator constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for river water containing both 1,2,3-trichloropropane and MTBE intended for groundwater recharge while balancing removal performance, operator simplicity, and moderate cost.

input_data.scenario_background: A water district plans to divert impacted river water to an infiltration basin for managed aquifer recharge. The source contains low-ug/L 1,2,3-trichloropropane and higher-level MTBE associated with mixed industrial and fuel-related upstream impacts. Several treatment routes are technically plausible, but the district has only moderate capital and operating budget, prefers straightforward operation, and wants to avoid highly specialized systems that are hard to run reliably with limited staff. The trade-off is that more aggressive air-stripping or membrane-based options can address MTBE or both contaminants, but their complexity, energy use, or inability to control TCP well enough make ranking dependent on overall constraint fit rather than contaminant removal alone.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Treated water should be suitable for groundwater recharge with reliable reduction of 1,2,3-trichloropropane and MTBE to support regulatory compliance and protect aquifer quality.
secondary_goals: provide stable performance despite variable river-water turbidity and organics
keep operation manageable for a utility with limited specialist staff
avoid treatment trains with disproportionately difficult residuals or concentrate management
limit total lifecycle cost to a moderate budget range

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer manageable spent media or conventional solids residuals; avoid difficult concentrate disposal and highly specialized hazardous residual handling where possible
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment should tolerate seasonal river-water quality variation and provide reliable contaminant control without constant operator retuning

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and part of natural organic matter to stabilize downstream treatment
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: polish clarified water and protect adsorption and UV reactors
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: provide strong adsorption of MTBE and reduce oxidant demand by lowering residual organics
core_unit_functions.uv_hydrogen_peroxide_advanced_oxidation: destroy low-level 1,2,3-trichloropropane and polish remaining dissolved organics

applicability_rationale: the combined route directly addresses the different treatability of the two target contaminants, with GAC favored for MTBE control and UV-based advanced oxidation used to address TCP where adsorption alone may be less reliable
pretreatment is well aligned with river water because solids and organic matter can interfere with both GAC run length and UV/AOP efficiency
placing GAC ahead of AOP can lower UV transmittance penalties and reduce overall oxidant demand compared with AOP on raw river water
this train balances treatment certainty with moderate operational complexity better than membrane or highly specialized destructive options
dual-bed GAC improves resilience to contaminant fluctuations and gives a more forgiving operating envelope for limited staff
potential_risks: AOP performance can decline if upstream TOC or UV254 rises significantly during storm events
hydrogen peroxide dosing and UV system maintenance require more operator attention than adsorption alone
spent carbon replacement or reactivation still needs planning
if MTBE loading increases materially, GAC bed life may shorten and OPEX can rise

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and bulk organic loading
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: protect downstream UV and carbon units
core_unit_functions.uv_hydrogen_peroxide_advanced_oxidation: oxidize 1,2,3-trichloropropane and partially transform other oxidizable dissolved organics
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_granular_activated_c: polish MTBE and biodegradable oxidation byproducts while providing final adsorption capacity

applicability_rationale: this is a technically strong integrated route because AOP is positioned to address TCP directly while downstream carbon provides finishing control for MTBE and byproducts
post-AOP GAC or biologically active carbon can improve finished-water stability and reduce concern about residual oxidation products
the sequence is attractive when regulatory confidence on TCP destruction is weighted heavily
it ranks slightly below the top option because running UV/AOP against higher dissolved organic background than after GAC can increase energy and peroxide demand
potential_risks: higher UV dose demand may occur when seasonal organic matter increases
bioactive carbon operation is manageable but still needs attention to headloss, biomass behavior, and media replacement
oxidation byproduct monitoring may be needed depending on water matrix

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and improve downstream reliability
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: polish clarified water before air and UV systems
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_aeration_or_diffused_air_st: remove volatile MTBE from water to air stream
core_unit_functions.uv_hydrogen_peroxide_advanced_oxidation: destroy remaining TCP that is not effectively addressed by stripping

applicability_rationale: air stripping is a recognized option for volatile gasoline-related compounds such as MTBE and can be effective when designed appropriately
the route separates the treatment duties clearly, with stripping for MTBE and AOP for TCP
it is a plausible alternative where GAC replacement cost is a concern and off-gas management is feasible
it ranks below adsorption-plus-AOP configurations because aeration systems and off-gas controls increase mechanical complexity and operator burden for a utility with limited staff
potential_risks: MTBE is more difficult to strip than some other volatile organics, so tower design and air-to-water ratio must be carefully selected
off-gas treatment may be required, adding cost and maintenance
air-stripping alone does not solve TCP control, so failure or underperformance of AOP leaves a compliance gap
scaling, fouling, and blower reliability can create more hands-on O&M than preferred

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce solids loading to protect membranes
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide broad dissolved contaminant reduction barrier including organics
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry before recharge use

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment offers a broad barrier and can be attractive when multiple dissolved contaminants are present
it is technically capable of reducing many trace organics and provides a conservative compliance-oriented approach

however, for this case it is downgraded because the source is river water requiring solid pretreatment, the operator base is limited, and concentrate management is misaligned with the project constraints
the broad treatment capability is not enough to outweigh the higher lifecycle complexity compared with integrated GAC and AOP options
potential_risks: concentrate disposal or management can be difficult and expensive
membrane fouling risk rises with seasonal river-water changes, increasing operator attention and chemical cleaning needs
stabilization and corrosion-related water chemistry management may be needed after treatment
this may be overbuilt relative to the contaminant problem and utility capability

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove bulk suspended solids and reduce some organic loading
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_single_stage: adsorb MTBE and some hydrophobic organic contaminants

applicability_rationale: this is operationally simple and often attractive for utilities with limited staff
GAC is a familiar, relatively robust treatment tool and can be effective for MTBE control under favorable loading conditions
it ranks last because it is not a reliable standalone solution for the dual-contaminant objective, especially for achieving robust TCP control in a recharge application
the option is included because it is superficially plausible and low in complexity, but the contaminant mix makes it poorly aligned with treatment certainty requirements
potential_risks: TCP breakthrough or incomplete removal is the main compliance risk
river-water organic matter can shorten GAC bed life and increase media change frequency
single-stage carbon provides less operational buffer against influent spikes than dual-stage systems
reliance on adsorption alone may not provide enough confidence for long-term recharge protection

preferred_rank_pattern: hybrid trains that explicitly address both MTBE and TCP should rank above single-technology options
pretreatment plus GAC plus AOP should rank near the top for balancing river-water matrix effects and operator limitations
membrane treatment should not rank first under moderate cost and limited-operator constraints
a technically plausible but incomplete low-complexity option should appear lower due to poor dual-contaminant fit

must_reflect: the need to treat two contaminants with different treatment behavior
moderate budget rather than unconstrained best-available treatment selection
limited operator specialization and preference for robust process trains
river-water solids and organic matter requiring practical pretreatment before polishing or destructive treatment

must_contain: why MTBE and TCP push toward different treatment mechanisms
why river-water turbidity and natural organic matter affect GAC and UV/AOP performance
why air stripping can be plausible for MTBE but insufficient alone for TCP
why membrane options are downgraded despite broad treatment capability

should_mention: AOP energy or peroxide demand sensitivity to organic matter
GAC bed life and breakthrough risk
off-gas handling or concentrate management burdens
the risk of choosing a simple train that does not adequately control TCP
